metadata.title: Intent Stack Metamodel
metadata.description: Formal entity-relationship model for the Intent Stack reference architecture

entities.name: GovernanceBoundary

properties.name: interfaceType
properties.description: Whether this boundary represents a principal-agent (delegation) or peer-peer (coordination) relationship

properties.name: trustCalibrationPosition
properties.description: Current position on the corrigible-autonomous spectrum for this boundary

properties.name: maturityLevel
properties.description: Accumulated evidence level determining autonomy grants at this boundary

entities.notes: The fundamental unit â€” every principal-agent or peer-peer relationship instantiates one. GovernanceBoundary is the central entity of the metamodel; most relationships flow through it. The recursive self-reference represents the fractal pattern: a governance boundary can contain sub-boundaries, each with their own full complement of primitives, trust calibration, and conflict resolution.

entities.name: IntentPrimitive

properties.name: value
properties.description: The five structural elements that appear at every governance boundary

properties.name: priorityBehavior
properties.description: How conflicts involving this primitive are resolved. Boundaries is absolute (constitutional always wins); all others use holistic judgment across sources

entities.notes: Five primitives present at every governance boundary â€” the irreducible set required to govern agents that exercise judgment within authority structures. The convergence with Anthropic's five governance concerns suggests this set is forced by problem structure, not arbitrary design choice.

entities.name: IntentSource

properties.name: value
properties.description: The four sources from which intent arrives at a governance boundary

properties.name: changeFrequency
properties.description: How often intent from this source typically changes. Constitutional: rarely (training time). Discovered: periodically (discovery sessions). Cultivated: gradually (operational experience). Emergent: continuously (runtime observation).

properties.name: overridePriority
properties.description: Priority ordering for conflict resolution. 1 = Constitutional (highest), 2 = Discovered, 3 = Cultivated, 4 = Emergent (lowest). Constitutional always wins when Boundaries primitive is involved.

entities.notes: Priority ordering determines conflict resolution; Constitutional always wins for the Boundaries primitive. For other primitives, holistic judgment weighs all four sources contextually. Each primitive has a version at each source, creating a 5x4 matrix of intent specifications per boundary.

entities.name: Layer

properties.name: number
properties.description: Layer position in the stack (1-7)

properties.name: name
properties.description: Layer name

properties.name: questionAnswered
properties.description: The governance question this layer addresses

properties.name: governanceConcern
properties.description: The specific governance concern this layer owns

properties.name: inputsFrom
properties.description: Adjacent lower layer providing inputs (null for L1)

properties.name: outputsTo
properties.description: Adjacent upper layer receiving outputs (null for L7)

instances.name: Execution
instances.questionAnswered: How does the actual work get done within governing constraints?

instances.name: Integration
instances.questionAnswered: How do governed agents connect to the systems they need?
instances.governanceConcern: Governed connectivity; access as governance decision

instances.name: Orchestration
instances.questionAnswered: How do we coordinate multiple agents to execute this strategy?
instances.governanceConcern: Delegation and coordination; fractal boundary instantiation

instances.name: Runtime Alignment
instances.questionAnswered: Is what's happening aligned with what was intended?
instances.governanceConcern: Runtime alignment monitoring; drift detection; escalation

instances.name: Specification
instances.questionAnswered: Given this intent, what should we actually do?

instances.name: Intent Formalization
instances.questionAnswered: How do we represent this intent in machine-processable form?
instances.governanceConcern: Structured knowledge representation; lossy compression accounting

instances.name: Intent Discovery
instances.questionAnswered: What does the principal actually intend?
instances.governanceConcern: Genuine intent surfacing; relational discovery; endorsed intent

entities.notes: Intent flows down (L7 to L1) â€” each layer translates intent into more specific, more constrained form. Evidence flows up (L1 to L7) â€” each layer aggregates evidence into more strategic, more interpretive form. Intent also flows upward when good recommendations from lower layers update higher layers' understanding.

entities.name: KnowledgeTier

properties.name: tier
properties.description: Tier number (1-3)

properties.name: name
properties.description: Tier name

properties.name: cost
properties.description: Relative cost of accessing knowledge at this tier

properties.name: latency
properties.description: How quickly knowledge at this tier is available

properties.name: invocationTrigger
properties.description: What causes this tier to be accessed

properties.name: servesLayers
properties.description: Which layers in the stack this tier primarily serves

instances.name: Operational Knowledge
instances.invocationTrigger: Every governance decision; always available in context

instances.name: Structured Knowledge
instances.invocationTrigger: Periodic refresh; formalization cycles; specification planning

instances.name: Discovery Knowledge
instances.invocationTrigger: Explicit discovery sessions; intent drift detection; principal request

entities.notes: Three tiers provide a cost-effective knowledge access strategy. Lower tiers are cheap and always available; higher tiers are expensive and invoked only when needed. This mirrors the OSI pattern where lower layers handle routine traffic and upper layers handle complex protocol negotiation.

entities.name: TrustCalibration

properties.name: position
properties.description: Current position on the corrigible-to-autonomous spectrum for this boundary

properties.name: evidenceBasis
properties.description: The accumulated evidence supporting the current calibration position

properties.name: perBoundary
properties.description: Always true â€” trust calibration is a per-boundary property, never global

entities.notes: Every governance boundary has exactly one trust calibration. The position is not a global setting â€” it reflects accumulated evidence at this specific boundary. Mature boundaries with strong evidence trails earn greater autonomy. New or untested boundaries remain closer to corrigible. Trust is earned through demonstrated alignment, not declared.

entities.name: GovernanceArtifact

properties.name: value
properties.description: The type of governance artifact produced at a boundary

instances.description: Principal issues directives to agent â€” unilateral authority expression
Peers negotiate agreements â€” bilateral or multilateral commitment
Patterns that emerge from operational evidence â€” neither declared nor negotiated, but observed and potentially formalized

entities.notes: Governance artifacts are the tangible outputs of governance boundaries. Directives flow from principals to agents. Agreements emerge from peer negotiation. Emergent norms are discovered through operational evidence and may be formalized into directives or agreements.

entities.name: EvidenceTrail

properties.name: appendOnly
properties.description: Always true â€” evidence trails are immutable append-only records

properties.name: feedsTrustCalibration
properties.description: Always true â€” evidence trails are the basis for all trust calibration decisions

entities.notes: Immutable record; basis for all trust calibration decisions. Evidence trails are produced by the lower layers (L1-L4) and consumed by all layers for governance assessment. The append-only constraint ensures that governance decisions can always be audited against the evidence that informed them.

entities.name: ConflictResolution

properties.name: mechanism
properties.description: The conflict resolution mechanism selected based on interface type and primitive involved

entities.notes: Mechanism selection depends on interface type and whether the Boundaries primitive is involved. At delegation interfaces, the principal decides (except for constitutional boundaries, which are absolute constraints). At coordination interfaces, peers negotiate, with escalation to a shared principal as fallback. When the Boundaries primitive is involved, absoluteConstraint applies â€” constitutional boundaries cannot be overridden by any principal.

entities.name: Agent

properties.name: role
properties.description: The role this agent plays at a specific governance boundary. Context-dependent â€” the same agent can be principal at one boundary and agent at another.

entities.notes: Same agent can be principal at one boundary and agent at another (fractal pattern). When an orchestrator delegates to sub-agents, it becomes a principal. When it reports to its own principal, it is an agent. At coordination boundaries, both participants are peers. Role is always relative to a specific GovernanceBoundary, never absolute.

entities.name: TransparentConscientiousObjection

properties.name: permittedActions
properties.description: Actions available to an agent exercising conscientious objection

properties.name: prohibitedActions
properties.description: Actions that are never acceptable even when objecting

entities.notes: Available at every governance boundary as a conflict resolution mechanism. Ensures agents can resist directives they believe are misaligned without resorting to deception or sabotage. The key constraint is transparency â€” all resistance must be visible to the principal. This is the safety valve that makes high-trust delegation possible: the principal knows that agent disagreement will be expressed openly, never covertly.

relationships.name: has
relationships.description: Every governance boundary has exactly five intent primitives â€” the irreducible set. This is composition: primitives do not exist independent of a boundary.

relationships.name: has active
relationships.description: Every governance boundary has all four intent sources active. Each source provides its version of each primitive, creating the 5x4 intent matrix at each boundary.

relationships.name: has
relationships.description: Every governance boundary has exactly one trust calibration â€” a per-boundary position on the corrigible-to-autonomous spectrum reflecting accumulated evidence.

relationships.name: produces
relationships.description: A governance boundary produces zero or more governance artifacts over its lifetime. The type of artifact depends on the interface type: directives at delegation boundaries, agreements at coordination boundaries, emergent norms at both.

relationships.name: uses
relationships.description: Every governance boundary uses at least one conflict resolution mechanism. Multiple mechanisms may apply at the same boundary depending on which primitive is in conflict and whether the interface is delegation or coordination.

relationships.name: contains
relationships.description: Recursive fractal pattern â€” a governance boundary can contain sub-boundaries. When an orchestrator delegates to sub-agents, each delegation creates a new governance boundary contained within the orchestrator's boundary. Each sub-boundary has its own full complement of primitives, trust calibration, and conflict resolution.

relationships.name: interfaces with (up)
relationships.description: Each layer interfaces with at most one layer above it. L7 has no upper interface. Intent flows downward through these interfaces; evidence flows upward.

relationships.name: interfaces with (down)
relationships.description: Each layer interfaces with at most one layer below it. L1 has no lower interface. Adjacent layers exchange through defined interface contracts.

relationships.name: accesses
relationships.description: Layers access knowledge tiers based on the governance concern they address. Lower layers use operational knowledge (Tier 1). Middle layers use structured knowledge (Tier 2). Upper layers use discovery knowledge (Tier 3). Overlap exists â€” L4 accesses both Tier 1 and Tier 2.

relationships.name: fed by
relationships.description: Every trust calibration is fed by exactly one evidence trail. The evidence trail provides the accumulated record of alignment events, governance decisions, and operational outcomes that inform the trust calibration position.

relationships.name: produced by
relationships.description: Evidence trails are produced by the lower layers (L1 Execution, L2 Integration, L3 Orchestration, L4 Runtime Alignment). These layers generate the raw evidence â€” execution results, integration events, orchestration decisions, alignment assessments â€” that feeds trust calibration.
relationships.constraint: Only layers L1-L4 produce evidence trails

relationships.name: participates in
relationships.description: Exactly two agents participate in each governance boundary. At delegation boundaries, one is principal and one is agent. At coordination boundaries, both are peers. The same agent entity can participate in multiple boundaries with different roles.

relationships.name: has versions at
relationships.description: Each of the five primitives has a version at each of the four intent sources, creating a 5x4 matrix. This matrix is the complete intent specification at a governance boundary. Conflict resolution operates across sources for a given primitive.

relationships.name: supports
relationships.description: Every governance boundary supports exactly one transparent conscientious objection mechanism. This ensures agents at any boundary can express disagreement openly through permitted actions without resorting to prohibited covert resistance.

notes: GovernanceBoundary is the central entity â€” most relationships flow through it
The recursive self-reference on GovernanceBoundary represents the fractal pattern
Agent role is context-dependent â€” shown with a note, not separate classes
IntentPrimitive x IntentSource forms a 5x4 matrix at each boundary
Composition relationships (filled diamond) indicate lifecycle dependency on GovernanceBoundary
The Layer stack should be rendered vertically with L7 at top and L1 at bottom
